content:
# Exodus Chapter 7 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios ~34v–35r (PDF pp. 67–68); Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 47–48 (LXX) | **Source Image:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_007_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_007_page_002.jpg`

---

**1** YHWH said to Moses, "See — I have made you like God to Pharaoh, and your brother Aaron will be your prophet.

**2** You will speak everything I command you, and Aaron your brother will tell Pharaoh to send the Israelites out of his land.

**3** But I will harden Pharaoh's heart, and I will multiply my signs and my wonders in the land of Egypt.

**4** Pharaoh will not listen to you. Then I will lay my hand on Egypt and bring out my armies — my people, the Israelites — from the land of Egypt with mighty acts of judgment.

**5** And the Egyptians will know that I am YHWH when I stretch out my hand against Egypt and bring the Israelites out from among them."

**6** Moses and Aaron did exactly as YHWH commanded them. They did it.

**7** Moses was eighty years old, and Aaron was eighty-three, when they spoke to Pharaoh.

**8** YHWH said to Moses and to Aaron,

**9** "When Pharaoh says to you, 'Prove yourselves — perform a wonder,' then say to Aaron, 'Take your staff and throw it down before Pharaoh,' and it will become a sea-serpent."

**10** So Moses and Aaron went to Pharaoh and did exactly as YHWH had commanded. Aaron threw down his staff before Pharaoh and before his officials, and it became a sea-serpent.

**11** Then Pharaoh summoned his own wise men and sorcerers, and they — the magicians of Egypt — did the same thing with their secret arts.

**12** Each man threw down his staff, and they became sea-serpents. But Aaron's staff swallowed theirs.

**13** Yet Pharaoh's heart grew rigid, and he did not listen to them — just as YHWH had said.

**14** Then YHWH said to Moses, "Pharaoh's heart is unyielding — he refuses to let the people go.

**15** Go to Pharaoh in the morning. He will be going out to the water. Station yourself to meet him on the bank of the Nile, and take in your hand the staff that turned into a serpent.

**16** Say to him: 'YHWH, the God of the Hebrews, sent me to you with this message: Let my people go, so they may serve me in the wilderness. But so far, you have not listened.

**17** This is how you will know that I am YHWH: Watch — I am about to strike the water of the Nile with the staff in my hand, and it will turn to blood.

**18** The fish in the Nile will die, and the river will reek, and the Egyptians will be unable to stomach drinking water from the Nile.'"

**19** Then YHWH said to Moses, "Tell Aaron: Take your staff and stretch out your hand over the waters of Egypt — over their rivers, their canals, their pools, every body of standing water — and they will become blood. There will be blood throughout the entire land of Egypt, even in vessels of wood and stone."

**20** Moses and Aaron did exactly as YHWH commanded. He raised the staff and struck the water of the Nile in the sight of Pharaoh and his officials, and all the water in the Nile turned to blood.

**21** The fish in the Nile died, and the river reeked, and the Egyptians could not drink water from the Nile. The blood was everywhere in the land of Egypt.

**22** But the magicians of Egypt did the same thing with their secret arts — and Pharaoh's heart grew rigid, and he did not listen to them, just as YHWH had said.

**23** Pharaoh turned and went into his palace, and he did not take even this to heart.

**24** All the Egyptians dug along the banks of the Nile for drinking water, because they could not drink from the river itself.

**25** Seven full days passed after YHWH struck the Nile.

---
## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**v.1 — "I have made you like God to Pharaoh" (Heb. *neta­tikha elohim le-Far'oh*, נְתַתִּ֥יךָ אֱלֹהִ֖ים לְפַרְעֹ֑ה):** This is the answer to Moses' double protest of inadequacy at 6:12 and 6:30. God does not fix Moses' lips; he redesigns the entire communication structure. The word *elohim* here does not mean "a god" in the polytheistic sense but "the authoritative one" — the one whose words carry ultimate weight. Pharaoh considered himself divine; YHWH now positions Moses as the divine figure in the room, with Aaron as his *navi* (prophet) — the one who speaks on his behalf, exactly as a prophet speaks for God. The LXX (Vaticanus) renders this as *theon* (θεόν), "god," preserving the startling claim. The irony is layered: the man who calls himself "uncircumcised of lips" (*aral sefatayim*) is now appointed to the role that Pharaoh claims for himself. PNEUMA retains "like God" rather than softening to "as a god" or "as God's representative" because the Hebrew intends the full force of the comparison.

**v.3 — "I will harden" (Heb. *aqsheh*, אַקְשֶׁ֖ה, from *qashah*):** This is the first explicit announcement of YHWH hardening Pharaoh's heart, and the verb is significant. The root *qashah* means "to be hard, rigid, severe" — it is the same root used for the "harsh labor" (*avodah qashah*) that crushed Israel. There is a bitter symmetry: the same rigidity Pharaoh imposed on Israel, God now imposes on Pharaoh's own heart. In the plague narratives, three different Hebrew verbs describe the hardening: (1) *chazaq* (חזק) — "to strengthen, to make firm" (the heart becomes resolute); (2) *kaved* (כבד) — "to make heavy" (the heart becomes dull, unresponsive); (3) *qashah* (קשה) — "to make rigid" (the heart becomes brittle and unyielding). Each carries a different nuance. Here in the programmatic announcement, God uses *qashah* — the harshest of the three. The Vaticanus uses *sklērunō* (σκληρυνῶ), "I will harden," matching the severity. PNEUMA will track each hardening verb throughout the plague cycle and render them distinctly.

**v.4 — "my armies — my people" (Heb. *tsiv'otai et-ammi*, צִבְאֹתַ֖י אֶת־עַמִּ֣י):** YHWH calls the Israelites *tsiv'otai* — "my armies" or "my hosts." These are slaves. They own nothing, carry no weapons, have no military training. Yet God names them as his fighting force. The word *tsava* (צבא) is military — it refers to organized troops, a marshaled host. The phrase looks forward to the entire Exodus event as a military campaign conducted by God himself, with a nation of slaves as his army. This is the theological root of the divine title *YHWH Tseva'ot* — "YHWH of Armies." The Vaticanus renders it *dunamin* (δύναμιν), "power/force," losing the military specificity.

**v.5 — "will know that I am YHWH" (Heb. *veya­de'u Mitsrayim ki-ani YHWH*, וְיָדְע֤וּ מִצְרַ֙יִם֙ כִּֽי־אֲנִ֣י יְהוָ֔ה):** The *recognition formula* — "they will know that I am YHWH" — is the theological thesis of the plague narrative. It appears repeatedly (7:5, 7:17, 8:22, 9:14, 9:29, 10:2, 11:7, 14:4, 14:18). The plagues are not random punishment; they are a curriculum. They are designed to produce *knowledge* — not intellectual assent but experiential recognition of who YHWH is. The subject here is striking: it is the *Egyptians* who will know. God's self-revelation is not limited to Israel. The Vaticanus preserves this: *gnōsontai pantes hoi Aiguptioi hoti egō eimi kurios* (γνώσονται πάντες οἱ Αἰγύπτιοι ὅτι ἐγώ εἰμι κύριος) — "all the Egyptians will know that I am the Lord."

**v.9 — "sea-serpent" (Heb. *tannin*, תַּנִּֽין):** This is not the *nachash* (נחש, "snake") of 4:3, where Moses' staff became a serpent in the wilderness. The word *tannin* refers to a great sea-creature — a dragon, a sea-monster, a chaos-beast. It appears in Genesis 1:21 (*tannin­im gedolim*, the great sea-creatures), in Isaiah 27:1 and 51:9 (the cosmic serpent Leviathan/Rahab), in Psalm 74:13, and in Ezekiel 29:3 and 32:2, where Pharaoh himself is called *hattannin haggadol* — "the great sea-serpent lurking in his rivers." The choice of *tannin* is not random. When Aaron's staff becomes a *tannin* before Pharaoh, it is a direct challenge to Pharaoh's cosmic pretensions. Egypt's king, who styled himself as the guardian of cosmic order (*ma'at*), now watches his own court magicians produce rival sea-serpents — only to see them swallowed. The Vaticanus uses *drakon* (δράκων), "dragon/serpent," preserving the mythic register. PNEUMA renders this "sea-serpent" throughout to distinguish it from the *nachash* of ch. 4 and to signal the cosmic dimension of the confrontation. This is not a parlor trick with snakes; it is a battle of cosmic symbols.

**v.11 — "secret arts" (Heb. *belaha­teihem*, בְּלַהֲטֵיהֶ֑ם):** The Hebrew word *lahat* (לַהַט) means "flame, blazing" — it is the same word used for the "flaming sword" (*lahat hacherev*, Gen. 3:24) that guards Eden. The magicians' arts are described with the language of supernatural fire — enchantments that burn, that dazzle, that obscure. Some scholars connect this to optical illusion or misdirection (the "flash" that distracts). The word appears again in v.22 and alternates with *belat­eihem* (בְּלָטֵיהֶ֗ם, from *lat*, "secrecy, hiddenness") in 8:3 and 8:14. PNEUMA uses "secret arts" as a dynamic rendering that captures both the concealment and the supernatural register without importing anachronistic terminology like "magic spells."

**v.12 — "Aaron's staff swallowed theirs" (Heb. *vayyivla matteh-Aharon et-mattotam*, וַיִּבְלַ֥ע מַטֵּֽה־אַהֲרֹ֖ן אֶת־מַטֹּתָֽם):** The verb *bala* (בלע) means "to swallow, to engulf" — the same verb used later when the earth swallows Korah's rebels (Num. 16:32) and when the sea swallows the Egyptian army (Exod. 15:12). Note carefully: the text says Aaron's *staff* swallowed their *staffs* — not that Aaron's serpent swallowed their serpents. The staff, the instrument of authority, devours the rivals. The LXX (Vaticanus) reads *katepien* (κατέπιεν), "swallowed down," with the same emphasis. This detail is the answer to the magicians' apparent parity: they can replicate the sign, but they cannot survive the confrontation. Their power is consumed.

**v.13 — "Pharaoh's heart grew rigid" (Heb. *vayyechezaq lev Par'oh*, וַיֶּחֱזַ֤ק לֵ֣ב פַּרְעֹ֔ה):** Here the verb is *chazaq* (חזק) — "to be strong, to become firm." This is distinct from *qashah* in v.3. In this first instance, the verb is in the Qal stem — Pharaoh's heart "grew strong/firm" on its own. The text does not say God hardened it here; Pharaoh's own heart stiffened. This is a critical distinction the hardening narrative maintains: in the early plagues, Pharaoh hardens his own heart; only later does God become the explicit agent of hardening (beginning with the sixth plague, boils, in 9:12). PNEUMA renders *chazaq* as "grew rigid" to distinguish it from *qashah* ("unyielding") and *kaved* ("heavy") when those appear later.

**v.14 — "unyielding" (Heb. *kaved*, כָּבֵ֖ד):** Now a second hardening verb appears: *kaved*, meaning "heavy, weighty, dull." Pharaoh's heart is *kaved* — heavy like a stone, unresponsive, sluggish. This is the adjective form (not a verb of divine action) — it describes the state of Pharaoh's heart as God diagnoses it. The same root gives us *kavod* ("glory, weight") — there is an ironic connection between the "glory" Pharaoh claims and the "heaviness" of the heart that will destroy him. The Vaticanus uses *bebarutai* (βεβάρηται), "has been made heavy," a perfect passive. PNEUMA renders this "unyielding" to distinguish it from the *chazaq*-hardening ("grew rigid") of v.13.

**v.15 — "He will be going out to the water" (Heb. *yotse hamay­mah*, יֹצֵ֣א הַמַּ֔יְמָה):** Pharaoh's morning journey to the Nile is the setting for the first plague, and the location is loaded with meaning. The Nile (*Ye'or*, יְאֹר, from the Egyptian *itrw*) was not merely a water source but a god — Hapi, the deity of the annual flood, the source of Egypt's life. Ancient Egyptian texts describe the Pharaoh performing ritual worship at the Nile. Whether this morning excursion is for bathing, ritual, or inspection, it places Pharaoh at the site of his kingdom's deepest religious claim: that the Nile's life-giving power validates Egypt's cosmic order. YHWH's first plague will strike precisely there.

**vv.17-18 — The Nile turns to blood (Heb. *venahafkhu ledam*, וְנֶהֶפְכ֖וּ לְדָֽם):** The first plague is not arbitrary. It targets the Nile — Egypt's source of life, agriculture, and sacred identity. The verb *hafakh* (הפך) means "to turn, to overturn, to transform" — the same verb used for the overthrow of Sodom (Gen. 19:21, 25). The water becomes *dam* (דם), "blood" — the fluid of death, the marker of violence. Fish die, the river stinks, and the water becomes undrinkable. For a civilization that depended entirely on the Nile, this is an assault on the foundation of existence. The Vaticanus adds *kai ta pota tous potamou apothanountai* — "the drinkable things of the river will die" — emphasizing the comprehensiveness of the destruction. PNEUMA renders *uva'ash* (וּבָאַ֣שׁ) as "reek" rather than "stink" to capture the nauseating, pervasive quality of the smell.

**v.19 — "even in vessels of wood and stone" (Heb. *uva'etsim uva'avanim*, וּבָעֵצִ֖ים וּבָאֲבָנִֽים):** This enigmatic phrase has generated extensive commentary. The most natural reading is that water stored in wooden and stone containers also turned to blood — meaning the plague was not confined to the river system but struck all water in Egypt, including reserves. The totality is the point: there is no escape, no stockpile that remains unaffected. The Vaticanus includes *en tois xulois kai en tois lithois* (ἐν τοῖς ξύλοις καὶ ἐν τοῖς λίθοις), preserving this detail.

**v.22 — The magicians replicate the plague:** This is the darkest absurdity of the plague narrative. The magicians of Egypt manage to replicate the Nile-to-blood plague with their secret arts. But think about what that means: they do not *reverse* it. They do not restore clean water. They make *more* blood. In a crisis of undrinkable water, the best Egypt's court sorcerers can do is make the problem worse. This is the narrator's devastating commentary on human power set against God's action: it can imitate, but it cannot heal. The Vaticanus preserves this with no editorial comment — the irony speaks for itself.

**v.23 — "he did not take even this to heart" (Heb. *velo-shat libbo gam-lazot*, וְלֹא־שָׁ֥ת לִבּ֖וֹ גַּם־לָזֹֽאת):** The verb *shut* (שׁית, here *shat*) means "to set, to place, to direct attention." Pharaoh did not *set his heart* — did not turn his attention, did not give consideration — *even to this*. The word *gam* ("even, also") implies that this is not the first thing Pharaoh has ignored. The narrator presents Pharaoh's retreat into his palace as a deliberate act of emotional withdrawal — he turns his back on the devastation and goes home. The Vaticanus reads *ouk epestēsen ton noun autou oude epi toutō* (οὐκ ἐπέστησεν τὸν νοῦν αὐτοῦ οὐδὲ ἐπὶ τούτῳ), "he did not turn his mind even to this."

**v.24 — Digging for water:** The verse is a snapshot of national desperation. The entire population of Egypt — not the rulers, not the magicians, but ordinary *Mitsrim* — must dig holes in the ground along the riverbank, hoping to find filtered groundwater. The plague has reduced the greatest civilization on earth to scrabbling in the dirt for something to drink. The narrative pauses on this image before moving on, letting it speak.

**v.25 — "Seven full days" (Heb. *shiv'at yamim*, שִׁבְעַ֣ת יָמִ֔ים):** The first plague lasts a complete cycle — seven days, the number of creation's fullness. YHWH's un-creation of Egypt's sacred river endures for one full week, a totality of time matching the totality of the plague's reach. The chapter ends not with resolution but with duration — the blood remains, the crisis continues, and Pharaoh is silent inside his palace.

**Hardening verb tracker for Exodus 7:**
| Verse | Hebrew Root | Stem | Agent | PNEUMA Rendering |
|-------|-----------|------|-------|-----------------|
| 7:3 | *qashah* (קשה) | Hiphil | YHWH (announced) | "harden" |
| 7:13 | *chazaq* (חזק) | Qal | Pharaoh's heart (self) | "grew rigid" |
| 7:14 | *kaved* (כבד) | Adjective | Description of state | "unyielding" |
| 7:22 | *chazaq* (חזק) | Qal | Pharaoh's heart (self) | "grew rigid" |

— *PNEUMA*
